problem text: Consider a continuous random variable X which is equally likely to take any real value between 1 and 2. Determine the variance of X.
problem solution: First, recall that for a uniform distribution over an interval [$$a$$, $$b$$], the variance is given by $$\dfrac{(b - a)^2}{12}$$. Since here $$a = 1$$ and $$b = 2$$, we substitute these into the variance formula to obtain: $$Var(X) = \dfrac{(2 - 1)^2}{12} = \dfrac{1}{12}.$$ Below is a more detailed derivation: We compute the mean $$E(X)$$ by integrating: $$E(X) = \int_{1}^{2} x \cdot f(x) dx = \int_{1}^{2} x \cdot 1 dx = \left[ \dfrac{x^2}{2} \right]_{1}^{2} = \dfrac{2^2}{2} - \dfrac{1^2}{2} = \dfrac{4}{2} - \dfrac{1}{2} = \dfrac{3}{2}.$$ Next, we find $$E(X^2)$$: $$E(X^2) = \int_{1}^{2} x^2 \cdot 1 dx = \left[ \dfrac{x^3}{3} \right]_{1}^{2} = \dfrac{2^3}{3} - \dfrac{1^3}{3} = \dfrac{8}{3} - \dfrac{1}{3} = \dfrac{7}{3}.$$ Hence, the variance $$Var(X)$$ is $$E(X^2) - [E(X)]^2 = \dfrac{7}{3} - \left( \dfrac{3}{2} \right)^2 = \dfrac{7}{3} - \dfrac{9}{4} = \dfrac{28}{12} - \dfrac{27}{12} = \dfrac{1}{12}.$$
problem hint: Compute the first and second moments of X (i.e., E(X) and E(X^2)) and use Var(X) = E(X^2) - (E(X))^2.
